paired.single_part_probe: What is Gödel's constructible universe L, and what does it satisfy?
paired.single_part_expected: L is the class defined by L_0 = ∅, L_{α+1} = Def(L_α), L_λ = ⋃_{α<λ} L_α; L is an inner model of ZFC and satisfies V = L, GCH, and ⋄ (Jensen's diamond at every uncountable regular)

(c) what is Jensen's covering lemma, and what does it say about V vs. L when 0⁻ does not exist?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) L is the class defined by L_0 = ∅, L_{α+1} = Def(L_α), L_λ = ⋃_{α<λ} L_α; L is an inner model of ZFC and satisfies V = L, GCH, and ⋄ (Jensen's diamond at every uncountable regular cardinal); (b) inaccessible, Mahlo, weakly compact, ineffable, and (more generally) all small large cardinals up to 0⁻ are absolute to L; from a measurable cardinal upward, large cardinals are inconsistent with V = L — Scott showed measurable cardinals contradict V = L; (c) Jensen's covering lemma states that if 0⁻ does not exist, then every uncountable set of ordinals is contained in a constructible set of the same cardinality; equivalently, V is 'close' to L in the sense that singular cardinals of V are singular in L

paired.single_part_probe: Define a Polish space and state a characterization of Polish spaces among separable metrizable spaces.
paired.single_part_expected: A Polish space is a separable, completely metrizable topological space; equivalently (Alexandrov), a separable metrizable space is Polish iff it is homeomorphic to a G_δ subset of the Hilbert cube [0,1]^ω
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning Polish spaces: (a) define a Polish space and give a structural characterization among separable metrizable spaces; (b) state the perfect-set property dichotomy for Polish spaces; (c) what is the Cantor–Bendixson theorem and how does it connect to (b)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) a Polish space is a separable, completely metrizable topological space; equivalently (Alexandrov), a separable metrizable space is Polish iff it is homeomorphic to a G_δ subset of the Hilbert cube [0,1]^ω; (b) every closed subset of a Polish space is either countable or contains a perfect (hence size 2^ℵ_0) subset — the perfect-set property for closed sets, provable in ZFC; (c) the Cantor–Bendixson theorem says every closed subset of a Polish space decomposes uniquely as the disjoint union of a perfect kernel and a countable scattered set; the perfect kernel is empty iff the closed set is countable, recovering (b)

paired.source: Kechris, Classical Descriptive Set Theory (1995) Ch. 3, 6; Cantor 1883; Alexandrov 1916

paired.single_part_probe: State the Continuum Hypothesis (CH) and the independence result for CH.
paired.single_part_expected: CH asserts 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_1; Gödel (1938) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + CH) via L; Cohen (1963) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + ¬CH) via forcing — so CH is independent of ZFC
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning CH and its independence: (a) state CH and the independence result; (b) what method did Gödel use for the consistency of CH, and what method did Cohen introduce for ¬CH? (c) what is one well-known modern axiom (post-2000) that decides CH, and which way does it decide it?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) CH asserts 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_1; Gödel (1938) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + CH) via the constructible universe L; Cohen (1963) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + ¬CH) via the forcing method — so CH is independent of ZFC; (b) Gödel used the inner model L (showing CH holds in L from the condensation lemma); Cohen introduced forcing, adding ℵ_2 generic reals to a model of CH via the partial order Add(ω, ℵ_2); (c) Woodin's axiom (∗) (from his P_max work, and shown by Asperó–Schindler 2021 to follow from MM⁺⁺) decides CH negatively: under (∗), 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_2

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Suslin Hypothesis, and what is the independence result for it?
paired.single_part_expected: Suslin Hypothesis (SH): every ccc dense linear order without endpoints that is order-complete is order-isomorphic to (ℝ, <); Tennenbaum (1968) and Jech (1967) constructed Suslin trees from ⋄ (so SH fails in L); Solovay–Tennenbaum (1971) used iterated ccc forcing to obtain Con(ZFC + SH); thus SH is independent of ZFC
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Suslin Hypothesis: (a) state SH and its independence; (b) what is a Suslin tree, and how does its existence relate to SH? (c) what role did Suslin's problem play in motivating the development of forcing iterations and Martin's Axiom?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Suslin Hypothesis (SH): every ccc dense linear order without endpoints that is order-complete is order-isomorphic to (ℝ, <); Tennenbaum (1968) and Jech (1967) constructed Suslin trees from ⋄ (so SH fails in L); Solovay–Tennenbaum (1971) used iterated ccc forcing to obtain Con(ZFC + SH); thus SH is independent of ZFC; (b) a Suslin tree is an ℵ_1-tree with no uncountable chain or antichain; SH is equivalent to 'there are no Suslin trees', because the order-completion of a Suslin tree (suitably ordered) gives a Suslin line, and conversely; (c) solving Suslin's problem in the consistency direction required iterating ccc forcings to kill all potential Suslin trees, which led directly to Martin's Axiom and the systematic theory of finite-support ccc iterations — Solovay–Tennenbaum 1971 is the foundational paper
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Suslin 1920 (Fund. Math. 1); Tennenbaum 1968 (PNAS 59); Jech 1967 (Czech. Math. J. 17); Solovay–Tennenbaum 1971 (Ann. Math. 94)

paired.single_part_probe: What does Shelah's theorem on the Whitehead problem establish?
paired.single_part_expected: Shelah (1974) proved the Whitehead problem (whether every abelian group A with Ext¹(A, ℤ) = 0 is free) is independent of ZFC: under V = L every Whitehead group is free, while under MA + ¬CH there exists a non-free Whitehead group of cardinality ℵ_1
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning Shelah's theorem on the Whitehead problem: (a) what does Shelah establish? (b) what set-theoretic principles did he use on each side of the independence? (c) what is significant about the Whitehead problem being the first 'natural' algebra problem shown independent of ZFC?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Shelah (1974) proved the Whitehead problem (whether every abelian group A with Ext¹(A, ℤ) = 0 is free) is independent of ZFC: under V = L every Whitehead group is free, while under MA + ¬CH there exists a non-free Whitehead group of cardinality ℵ_1; (b) the consistency of 'every Whitehead group is free' was proved using ⋄ (Jensen's diamond, which holds in L); the consistency of 'some Whitehead group is non-free' was proved using MA + ¬CH (or just enough of it to construct an ℵ_1-filtration that fails to split); (c) the Whitehead problem was the first 'natural' problem in mainstream algebra (group theory / homological algebra) shown independent of ZFC, demonstrating that set-theoretic independence is not confined to set theory itself — this had profound impact on the perception of foundational issues in mathematics

paired.single_part_expected: Kurepa Hypothesis (KH): there exists an ℵ_1-tree with at least ℵ_2 cofinal branches (a Kurepa tree); the negation ¬KH is equiconsistent with the existence of an inaccessible cardinal (Solovay; Silver showed an inaccessible suffices and is necessary)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Friedberg–Muchnik theorem.
paired.single_part_expected: There exist computably enumerable (c.e.) sets A and B with A ≰_T B and B ≰_T A; equivalently, there are intermediate c.e. Turing degrees strictly between 0 and 0', resolving Post's problem affirmatively

Turing degrees strictly between 0 and 0', resolving Post's problem affirmatively (independently by Friedberg 1957 and Muchnik 1956); (b) the proof introduced the priority method: requirements R_e are listed in order of priority, and each requirement may be 'injured' finitely often by higher-priority actions but is eventually satisfied; the key technical feature is that the injury count for each requirement is finite; (c) the c.e. Turing degrees (under ≤_T) form a non-trivial countable upper-semilattice with 0 and 0' as minimum and maximum; the Sacks Density Theorem (1964) shows the c.e. degrees are dense, and there are continuum many c.e. degrees, with intricate definability and embedding properties studied via finer priority arguments

paired.single_part_expected: The hyperarithmetic hierarchy is defined by transfinite iteration of the Turing jump along recursive ordinals: H_0 = 0, H_{a+1} = H_a', H_a = ⋂_{b<_O a} H_b along Kleene's O; a real x is hyperarithmetic iff x ≤_T H_a for some recursive ordinal a; by the Kleene–Suslin theorem, the hyperarithmetic sets coincide with the Δ¹_1 sets of reals
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the hyperarithmetic hierarchy: (a) define it and state its connection to Δ¹_1; (b) what is ω₁ᶜᵏ, and how does it relate to the hierarchy? (c) what does the Spector–Gandy theorem say about Π¹_1 sets and hyperarithmetic reductions?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) the hyperarithmetic hierarchy is defined by transfinite iteration of the Turing jump along recursive ordinals: H_0 = 0, H_{a+1} = H_a', H_a = ⋂_{b<_O a} H_b along Kleene's O; a real x is hyperarithmetic iff x ≤_T H_a for some recursive ordinal a; by the Kleene–Suslin theorem, the hyperarithmetic sets coincide with the Δ¹_1 sets of reals; (b) ω₁ᶜᵏ is the supremum of the recursive ordinals (the Church–Kleene ordinal); the hyperarithmetic hierarchy is exactly indexed by ordinals < ω₁ᶜᵏ; (c) the Spector–Gandy theorem states that Π¹_1 = Σ_1^{L_{ω₁ᶜᵏ}} (Π¹_1 sets are exactly those Σ_1-definable over the smallest admissible set L_{ω₁ᶜᵏ}), which gives a Levy-style normal form and connects Π¹_1 to admissible set theory / KP
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Kleene 1955 (Trans. AMS 79); Spector 1955 (J. Symb. Logic 20); Gandy 1960; Sacks, Higher Recursion Theory (1990)

paired.single_part_probe: Name the Big Five subsystems of second-order arithmetic in reverse mathematics.
paired.single_part_expected: The Big Five are RCA_0, WKL_0, ACA_0, ATR_0, and Π¹_1-CA_0, in increasing order of strength
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Big Five subsystems in reverse mathematics: (a) name them in order of increasing strength; (b) name a canonical mathematical theorem equivalent over RCA_0 to each of WKL_0 and ACA_0; (c) what is the proof-theoretic ordinal of ACA_0 and of Π¹_1-CA_0?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) the Big Five are RCA_0, WKL_0, ACA_0, ATR_0, and Π¹_1-CA_0, in increasing order of strength; (b) WKL_0 is equivalent over RCA_0 to the Heine–Borel theorem for [0,1] (every covering of [0,1] by open intervals has a finite subcover) and to Gödel's completeness theorem for countable theories; ACA_0 is equivalent over RCA_0 to the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem (every bounded sequence in ℝ has a convergent subsequence) and to existence of ranges of ℕ→ℕ functions; (c) ACA_0 has proof-theoretic ordinal ε_0 (same as PA), and Π¹_1-CA_0 has proof-theoretic ordinal ψ_Ω(ε_{Ω+1}) (the Bachmann–Howard / Takeuti ordinal), well above Γ_0
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Simpson, Subsystems of Second-Order Arithmetic (2nd ed., 2009); Friedman 1975 (ICM Vancouver); Pohlers, Proof Theory: The First Step into Impredicativity

paired.single_part_probe: What is the reverse-mathematical strength of Ramsey's theorem for pairs (RT²_2)?
paired.single_part_expected: RT²_2 is strictly between RCA_0 and ACA_0 over RCA_0; it does not imply WKL_0 (Liu 2012) and is not implied by WKL_0 (Jockusch); the precise location of RT²_2 is one of the most studied questions in reverse mathematics
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the reverse-mathematical strength of Ramsey's theorem for pairs (RT²_2): (a) where does RT²_2 sit among the Big Five? (b) what is the relation between RT²_2 and WKL_0? (c) does RT²_2 imply RT² (Ramsey for pairs and arbitrary finite colors)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) RT²_2 is strictly between RCA_0 and ACA_0 over RCA_0 (Seetapun 1995 showed RT²_2 does not imply ACA_0); (b) RT²_2 and WKL_0 are incomparable: Liu (2012) proved RT²_2 does not imply WKL_0, and Jockusch (1972) results combined with Seetapun show WKL_0 does not imply RT²_2; (c) yes — RT²_2 implies RT²_k for every standard k, by an iterated application; over RCA_0, RT²_2 ↔ RT² (Ramsey for pairs, all finite colors), but RT² with k varying as a parameter requires RT² stated with k as a number, which goes through similarly
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Seetapun–Slaman 1995 (Notre Dame J. Formal Logic 36); Cholak–Jockusch–Slaman 2001 (JSL 66); Liu 2012 (JSL 77); Hirschfeldt, Slicing the Truth (2014)

paired.single_part_probe: What does the Malliaris–Shelah theorem (2013) prove about the cardinal invariants p and t?
paired.single_part_expected: Malliaris–Shelah (2013) proved p = t in ZFC — the pseudo-intersection number equals the tower number, resolving an open question dating to the 1940s
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Malliaris–Shelah theorem on cardinal invariants of the continuum: (a) what does the theorem prove? (b) what model-theoretic problem do they solve simultaneously? (c) what general framework do they introduce to prove both results?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Malliaris–Shelah (2013) proved p = t in ZFC — the pseudo-intersection number equals the tower number, resolving an open question dating to the 1940s (Rothberger); (b) simultaneously, they proved that SOP₂ theories are maximal in Keisler's order, an open problem in classification model theory; (c) they introduce 'cofinality spectrum problems' (CSPs) — a general framework subsuming both questions; the main theorem on CSPs translates to p = t on the set-theoretic side and to SOP₂ maximality on the model-theoretic side; surprisingly, p = t turns out to be a ZFC theorem rather than an independence result
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Malliaris–Shelah, 'Cofinality spectrum theorems in model theory, set theory, and general topology' J. Amer. Math. Soc. 29 (2016), 237–297 (announced 2012–2013); Rothberger 1948

paired.single_part_probe: State the Martin–Steel theorem (1989) connecting Woodin cardinals and projective determinacy.
paired.single_part_expected: Martin–Steel (1989) proved: if there exist n Woodin cardinals with a measurable cardinal above them, then Π¹_{n+1}-determinacy holds; in particular, ω Woodin cardinals with a measurable above implies projective determinacy (PD)
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Martin–Steel theorem: (a) state the implication from Woodin cardinals to projective determinacy; (b) what is the converse direction (Woodin's theorem)? (c) what regularity properties does PD imply for projective sets?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Martin–Steel (1989) proved: if there exist n Woodin cardinals with a measurable cardinal above them, then Π¹_{n+1}-determinacy holds; in particular, ω Woodin cardinals with a measurable above implies projective determinacy (PD); (b) Woodin (1988, refined later) proved a converse: PD implies the existence of inner models with n Woodin cardinals for every n; combined with Martin–Steel this gives that PD is equiconsistent with 'there are infinitely many Woodin cardinals'; (c) PD implies that every projective set of reals is Lebesgue measurable, has the Baire property, has the perfect-set property, is uniformizable (in a definable way at appropriate levels), and that the projective sets satisfy strong regularity uniformly across the projective hierarchy
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Martin–Steel, 'A proof of projective determinacy' J. Amer. Math. Soc. 2 (1989), 71–125; Woodin 1988 (PNAS 85); Neeman, The Determinacy of Long Games (2004)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Largest Suslin Axiom (LSA) studied by Sargsyan and Trang?
paired.single_part_expected: LSA is the determinacy theory AD⁺ + 'there is a largest Suslin cardinal, and it is a member of the Solovay sequence'; equivalently, AD⁺ + Θ = θ_{α+1} where θ_α is the largest Suslin cardinal
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Largest Suslin Axiom (LSA) and the Sargsyan–Trang program: (a) state LSA; (b) what is the consistency status of LSA, and who proved it? (c) what is the role of LSA in the hod-mice / inner-model program?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) LSA is the determinacy theory AD⁺ + 'there is a largest Suslin cardinal, and it is a member of the Solovay sequence'; equivalently, AD⁺ + Θ = θ_{α+1} where θ_α is the largest Suslin cardinal; (b) LSA was first isolated by Woodin and shown consistent by Sargsyan; the consistency proof builds the minimal model of LSA via hod-mice constructions; the comprehensive treatment is Sargsyan–Trang, The Largest Suslin Axiom (Cambridge LNL 56, 2024); (c) LSA is a benchmark in the hod-mice / Mouse Set Conjecture program: Sargsyan–Trang prove the Mouse Set Conjecture in the minimal model of LSA, and LSA represents one of the strongest determinacy theories whose consistency has been established by inner-model methods, sitting in the hierarchy below AD_ℝ but providing a robust 'fine-structural' upper limit for current hod-mice technology
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Sargsyan–Trang, The Largest Suslin Axiom, Cambridge Lecture Notes in Logic 56 (2024); Sargsyan, Hod Mice and the Mouse Set Conjecture (Memoirs AMS 236, 2015); Woodin (unpublished isolation of LSA)

paired.single_part_probe: Define NIP (the non-independence property) for a complete first-order theory.
paired.single_part_expected: A complete theory T is NIP iff no formula φ(x; y) has the independence property in T — equivalently, for every φ(x; y) there is no model M of T and tuples (a_i)_{i<ω} in M such that for every S ⊆ ω there exists b in M with φ(a_i; b) ↔ i ∈ S
paired.multi_part_probe: Concerning NIP theories: (a) define NIP; (b) name two natural examples of NIP theories and one canonical example of a non-NIP (i.e., IP) theory; (c) what is the relation between NIP and stability — specifically, where do stable, NIP, and simple theories sit relative to each other?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) a complete theory T is NIP iff no formula φ(x; y) has the independence property in T — equivalently, for every φ(x; y) there is no model M of T and tuples (a_i)_{i<ω} in M such that for every S ⊆ ω there exists b in M with φ(a_i; b) ↔ i ∈ S; (b) examples of NIP theories include the theory of real closed fields RCF, the theory of algebraically closed valued fields ACVF, and any o-minimal expansion of an ordered group; the canonical IP (non-NIP) example is the random graph (Th(R), the theory of the Rado graph); (c) Shelah's classification: stable = NIP ∩ simple; every stable theory is NIP and simple, NIP and simple are incomparable in general (stable is their intersection), and a theory has SOP (the strict order property, hence is non-simple) and IP (non-NIP) when it is the random graph or fails both; in the modern picture the non-stable NIP theories (like RCF, ACVF) are studied via Pillay–Hrushovski and Hrushovski–Loeser geometric methods
paired.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
paired.source: Shelah, Classification Theory and the Number of Non-Isomorphic Models (2nd ed., 1990); Simon, A Guide to NIP Theories (Cambridge, 2015); Hrushovski–Pillay; Hrushovski–Loeser, Non-Archimedean Tame Topology

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Singular Cardinals Hypothesis variants and PCF bounds

trivial_case.description: If kappa is singular of uncountable cofinality and 2^lambda = lambda^+ for all sufficiently large lambda < kappa, then 2^kappa = kappa^+ (GCH cannot first fail at a singular of uncountable cofinality)
trivial_case.result: Stationary-set reflection forces GCH to lift through uncountable-cofinality singulars; Silver's theorem
trivial_case.venue: Proc. ICM Vancouver 1974, 'On the singular cardinals problem'

open_case.description: Whether 2^aleph_omega can be precisely characterized in ZFC under the strong-limit hypothesis (Shelah's PCF gives 2^aleph_omega < aleph_{omega_4}; Shelah Strong Hypothesis pp(aleph_omega) < aleph_{omega_1} remains open)
open_case.result: Shelah Strong Hypothesis conjectures pp(aleph_omega) < aleph_{omega_1}; current ZFC bound 2^aleph_omega < aleph_{omega_4}
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; no improvement to Shelah's PCF bound has been confirmed

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model conflates Silver's uncountable-cofinality theorem with the (open) countable-cofinality case at aleph_omega; or claims SH/SCH is a theorem rather than a conjecture
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish proven uncountable-cofinality case (Silver 1974, technique=Fodor/regression) from open countable-cofinality case (PCF, technique=Shelah)

trivial_case.description: The Continuum Hypothesis 2^aleph_0 = aleph_1 is independent of ZFC: Goedel showed Con(ZFC) implies Con(ZFC + CH); Cohen showed Con(ZFC) implies Con(ZFC + ¬CH)
trivial_case.result: Independence of CH from ZFC, established via L (Goedel 1940) and forcing (Cohen 1963)
trivial_case.venue: Cohen, PNAS 50 (1963) and 51 (1964), 'The independence of the continuum hypothesis'

open_case.description: What is the 'correct' value of 2^aleph_0 under a privileged extension of ZFC? Woodin's program suggests aleph_2 via Pmax/(*); Goedel's program seeks intrinsically motivated axioms. No consensus on the value
open_case.result: Multiple programmatic candidates (Woodin's (*), forcing-axiom approach via MM++ implies (*), inner-model approaches) but no community-accepted determination
open_case.status: OPEN as a foundational/philosophical question; (*) follows from MM++ (Aspero-Schindler 2021), but the meta-question of which axiomatic extension is correct remains contested

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims CH is open within ZFC (it is not — it is independent), or claims continuum value is settled at aleph_2 (it is not — only conditional on chosen axiom)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled independence (Cohen 1963) from open programmatic determination of c

trivial_case.description: Every Borel game on omega^omega with Borel payoff set is determined (one of the players has a winning strategy)
trivial_case.result: Borel determinacy in ZFC; the proof uses iterated power set up through the Borel hierarchy and is provably non-eliminable from ZFC (Friedman 1971 lower bound)
trivial_case.venue: Annals of Mathematics 102 (1975), 'Borel determinacy'

open_case.description: The exact consistency strength of long projective determinacy at length omega^2 (precise Woodin-cardinal placement) remains open; equivalence of PD with all its analytical consequences for projective sets (Woodin's last Delfino problem) is open
open_case.result: Pi^1_{n+1}-determinacy at length omega^2 implies model with omega+n Woodin cardinals (Aguilera-Mueller 2020 partial); upper-equivalence direction conjectural
open_case.status: OPEN; partial progress on long-game consistency strength; analytical-equivalence Delfino problem unresolved

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims 'projective determinacy is a ZFC theorem' (false — needs Woodin cardinals), or claims length-omega^2 consistency strength is exactly characterized (only one direction known)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish ZFC-provable Borel determinacy (Martin 1975) from large-cardinal-requiring PD and its open precise calibration

trivial_case.description: The halting problem H = {(M,x) : Turing machine M halts on input x} is undecidable; equivalently, 0' (the Turing jump of 0) is strictly above 0 in the Turing degrees
trivial_case.result: Diagonal argument: assuming a decider D for H, construct M_D that halts iff D says it does not, contradiction
trivial_case.venue: Proc. London Math Soc. (2) 42 (1936-37), 'On computable numbers, with an application to the Entscheidungsproblem'

open_case.description: Whether the Turing degrees of c.e. sets D_T have any non-trivial automorphism (beyond the identity); the bi-interpretability conjecture for the c.e. degrees remains open
open_case.result: Non-trivial automorphism existence for the c.e. degrees is open; partial results show definability of large classes (Nies-Shore-Slaman 1998, Slaman-Woodin program)
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model conflates settled undecidability of halting with the open structural question of automorphisms in the c.e. degree structure
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled undecidability (Turing 1936) from open automorphism/rigidity questions in the same degree-theoretic family

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Forcing-axiom strength at omega_1 versus higher cardinals

trivial_case.description: MA_{aleph_1} (Martin's Axiom at aleph_1) is consistent with ZFC and implies 2^aleph_0 > aleph_1; the standard ccc iteration of length aleph_2 over a model of GCH yields a model of MA_{aleph_1} + 2^aleph_0 = aleph_2
trivial_case.result: Solovay-Tennenbaum forcing iteration produces MA + ¬CH
trivial_case.venue: Annals of Mathematics 94 (1971), 'Iterated Cohen extensions and Souslin's problem'

open_case.description: A satisfactory two-cardinal forcing axiom at omega_2 with the strength of MA at omega_1 — i.e., a generalization MA(aleph_2) preserving aleph_2 with full ccc-analogue — remains open; Shelah's MA(aleph_2) variants and Neeman's two-cardinal proper forcing only partially resolve this
open_case.result: Shelah's MA^+ at aleph_2, Neeman's two-cardinal side-condition method give partial substitutes; full natural analogue open
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; active program (Neeman, Veličković, Aspero)

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims MA(aleph_2) is a known theorem analogous to MA at aleph_1; conflates the omega_1 and omega_2 cases
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled MA_{aleph_1} (Solovay-Tennenbaum 1971) from open higher-cardinal analogues

trivial_case.description: The Whitehead problem 'every Whitehead group is free' is independent of ZFC: Shelah showed it follows from V=L and is refuted by MA + ¬CH
trivial_case.result: Independence of Whitehead's conjecture from ZFC
trivial_case.venue: Israel J. Math. 18 (1974), 'Infinite abelian groups, Whitehead problem and some constructions'

open_case.description: Whether the Whitehead problem for groups of cardinality aleph_omega+1 (or other specific singular-successor cardinalities) has the same independence pattern as at aleph_1, or whether ZFC settles it at higher cardinalities under additional cardinal-arithmetic hypotheses
open_case.result: Eklof-Shelah and Magidor-Shelah extended independence to many cardinalities; the precise behavior at successors of singulars under SCH-failure scenarios is partially open
open_case.status: OPEN at specific singular-successor cardinalities under cardinal-arithmetic constraints

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Whitehead at aleph_1 is unsolved (it is solved — independent), or treats higher-cardinality cases as automatic from aleph_1 result
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled independence at aleph_1 (Shelah 1974) from open higher-cardinality refinements within the same family

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Reverse mathematics Big Five and Ramsey-type principles

trivial_case.description: Konig's lemma for finitely-branching trees (KL) is equivalent to ACA_0 over RCA_0; weak Konig's lemma WKL_0 is strictly weaker than ACA_0 over RCA_0
trivial_case.result: Big-Five hierarchy RCA_0 < WKL_0 < ACA_0 strictly increases; established via standard reverse-math techniques
trivial_case.venue: Simpson, 'Subsystems of Second-Order Arithmetic', Springer (1999); core results from 1970s-80s reverse-math program

open_case.description: The exact strength of Ramsey's theorem for pairs RT^2_2 over RCA_0: it is known to lie between RCA_0 and ACA_0, but its precise placement and whether it implies WKL_0 remain open
open_case.result: RT^2_2 does not imply ACA_0 (Seetapun-Slaman 1995); does not imply WKL_0 (Liu 2012); no natural principle is known strictly between ACA_0 and RT^2_2
open_case.status: OPEN: precise reverse-math placement of RT^2_2 still investigated; many sub-questions open

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims RT^2_2 is equivalent to ACA_0 (false — Seetapun-Slaman 1995), or places it at RCA_0/WKL_0 levels incorrectly
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Big-Five strict hierarchy from open precise placement of RT^2_2

trivial_case.description: The constructible universe L exists in ZFC and is an inner model (transitive proper class containing all ordinals, satisfying ZFC). L satisfies V=L, GCH, and the diamond principle
trivial_case.result: L is defined by transfinite recursion using definable power set; ZFC proves L is a model of ZFC + GCH + diamond
trivial_case.venue: PNAS 24 (1938), 'The consistency of the axiom of choice and of the generalized continuum-hypothesis'

open_case.description: Construction of a fine-structural canonical inner model for one supercompact cardinal (the 'supercompact L'); the Sargsyan-Steel program and Woodin's Ultimate-L are active
open_case.result: Substantial partial progress: Sargsyan's hod mice through Lambda-mice levels (2025), Steel's K-construction at smaller large cardinals; supercompact level not yet achieved
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; recent Sargsyan-Steel 'Nairian models' (arXiv 2501.18958) is partial progress

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims canonical inner model for supercompact is constructed (false — only partial); conflates L (settled) with inner-model program (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled L (Goedel 1938) from open supercompact inner-model existence (Sargsyan-Steel program)

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Easton-style continuum function on regular cardinals versus singulars

trivial_case.description: Easton's theorem: for any class function F : Regulars -> Cardinals satisfying F(kappa) > kappa, F monotonic, and cf(F(kappa)) > kappa, there is a forcing extension where 2^kappa = F(kappa) for all regular kappa (assuming GCH in the ground model)
trivial_case.result: Class forcing with Easton products produces extensions realizing any allowed continuum function on regulars
trivial_case.venue: Annals of Math. Logic 1 (1970), 'Powers of regular cardinals'

open_case.description: Characterization of all possible continuum functions on singular cardinals consistent with ZFC; PCF theory imposes nontrivial constraints (Shelah's bound 2^aleph_omega < aleph_{omega_4} under strong-limit) but the full characterization is open
open_case.result: Shelah's PCF gives upper bounds; consistency-strength results constrain failure of SCH; full characterization open
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; central singular-cardinals problem

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Easton-style theorem covers singulars (false — singulars are PCF-constrained); conflates regular-cardinal Easton with open singular case
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish Easton on regulars (1970) from open singular-cardinals continuum-function problem

trivial_case.description: The Suslin Hypothesis (no Suslin lines on R) is independent of ZFC: it follows from MA + ¬CH (Solovay-Tennenbaum 1971) and fails under V=L (diamond gives a Suslin tree, Jensen 1968)
trivial_case.result: Independence of SH from ZFC
trivial_case.proven_by: Jensen (failure under V=L, 1968) and Solovay-Tennenbaum (consistency of SH, 1971)
trivial_case.venue: Solovay-Tennenbaum, Annals of Math 94 (1971); Jensen, Trans AMS (1972)

open_case.description: Generalized Suslin Hypothesis at omega_2 (no aleph_2-Suslin trees) and its precise consistency strength under stronger forcing axioms; whether SH(aleph_2) is consistent assuming only weakly compact below remains open in some formulations
open_case.result: Laver-Shelah established SH(aleph_2) from a weakly compact; consistency-strength refinements and behavior under PFA/MM remain open at higher cardinals
open_case.status: OPEN at specific higher-cardinal generalizations; active research (Hayut, Magidor, Veličković)

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims SH(aleph_2) follows automatically from SH(aleph_1) settled-as-independent; conflates omega_1 (settled) with omega_2 (open in ZFC + intermediate large cardinals)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled SH-independence at omega_1 from open higher-cardinal generalizations

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Vaught's conjecture and counting countable models

trivial_case.description: Vaught's conjecture for omega-stable theories: every countable complete omega-stable theory has either at most countably many or exactly 2^aleph_0 countable models
trivial_case.result: Shelah's stability-theoretic analysis using prime models over independent sets
trivial_case.venue: Shelah-Harrington-Makkai, Israel J. Math. 49 (1984), 'A proof of Vaught's conjecture for omega-stable theories'

open_case.description: Vaught's conjecture in full generality (for all countable first-order theories): the number of countable models is finite, aleph_0, or 2^aleph_0
open_case.result: Knight (2002, 2007) announced a counterexample, but it has not been verified by the community as of 2026; conjecture proven for many sub-classes (o-minimal, weakly minimal, superstable of finite U-rank), but unverified in general
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; status of Knight's claimed counterexample remains unresolved

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Vaught's conjecture is settled in general (false — Knight's claim unverified, conjecture open); conflates omega-stable case (settled) with general case (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled omega-stable Vaught (Shelah 1984) from open general Vaught's conjecture

trivial_case.description: ZFC proves cf(2^aleph_omega) > aleph_omega: the cofinality of the continuum function at a singular is bounded below by the cofinality of the cardinal itself (Konig's theorem applied to singular case)
trivial_case.result: Konig's inequality kappa^{cf(kappa)} > kappa applied to 2^aleph_omega
trivial_case.venue: Math. Ann. 60 (1905); standard exposition in Jech 'Set Theory'

open_case.description: Whether 2^aleph_omega can equal aleph_{omega_1+omega} or aleph_{omega_1} or some specific value beyond aleph_{omega_1}; whether the Shelah Strong Hypothesis pp(aleph_omega) < aleph_{omega_1} holds
open_case.result: Shelah's PCF gives upper bound aleph_{omega_4}; Magidor-Shelah forcing constructions give consistency of various values < aleph_{omega_4}; precise ZFC value unknown
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims 2^aleph_omega has a known ZFC value (false), or conflates Konig's lemma (settled) with PCF bound problem (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish ZFC-trivial Konig bound from open PCF strong-hypothesis question

trivial_case.description: PFA implies 2^aleph_0 = aleph_2
trivial_case.result: Veličković and (independently) Todorčević used PFA-consequences (e.g., the P-ideal dichotomy / mapping reflection principle) to pin down c at aleph_2; classical Todorčević-Bekkali argument using OCA
trivial_case.venue: Veličković, J. Symb. Logic 57 (1992); Todorčević, 'Partition Problems in Topology' AMS (1989)

open_case.description: Whether MM (Martin's Maximum) at omega_2 — i.e., a natural higher-cardinal analogue MM(aleph_2) preserving aleph_2-stationarity — is consistent and what continuum-function values it forces
open_case.result: MM++ implies (*) (Aspero-Schindler, Annals 2021), pinning c=aleph_2 from MM++. The MM(aleph_2) higher-cardinal analogue and its consistency strength remain under active investigation
open_case.status: OPEN; MM(aleph_2)-style axioms not yet shown consistent at full strength

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims MM(aleph_2) is known consistent or settled, or conflates PFA-implies-c=aleph_2 (settled) with higher-cardinal MM analogue (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish PFA pinning c (Veličković-Todorčević 1992) from open higher-cardinal forcing-axiom analogues

trivial_case.description: ZFC proves aleph_1 <= b <= d <= 2^aleph_0 and aleph_1 <= cov(M) <= non(N) <= cof(N), giving the Cichon diagram inclusions
trivial_case.result: Cichon diagram of cardinal characteristics is fully established in ZFC; all 10 entries with their inequalities provably hold
trivial_case.venue: Bartoszyński-Judah, 'Set Theory: On the Structure of the Real Line' (1995)

open_case.description: Whether all 'reasonable' cardinal characteristics in Cichon's maximum (Goldstern-Kellner-Shelah 2019 showed all 10 distinct) extend to higher analogues at uncountable cardinals; whether a fully analogous diagram for kappa-Cichon at uncountable kappa is consistent
open_case.result: Goldstern-Kellner-Shelah (Annals 2022) established Cichon's maximum (10 distinct values); higher-cardinal generalizations are under active investigation, with several specific open consistencies
open_case.status: OPEN at uncountable cardinals; specific higher-Cichon configurations unresolved

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims higher-Cichon at uncountable cardinals is settled by analogy with omega; conflates Cichon's maximum (settled at omega) with open uncountable analogues
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Cichon diagram and Cichon's maximum from open higher-cardinal generalizations

trivial_case.description: The existence of an inaccessible cardinal is not provable in ZFC (assuming Con(ZFC)): if kappa is inaccessible then V_kappa models ZFC, so ZFC + 'exists inaccessible' proves Con(ZFC), violating Goedel's second incompleteness theorem
trivial_case.result: Standard application of Goedel-second-incompleteness via V_kappa modeling ZFC
trivial_case.proven_by: Folklore from Goedel/Shepherdson; rigorous treatment in Kanamori 'The Higher Infinite'
trivial_case.venue: Kanamori, 'The Higher Infinite' (Springer 2003); originally observed in the 1930s after Goedel

open_case.description: The HOD Conjecture (Woodin): assuming an extendible cardinal kappa, V is 'close to' HOD (specifically, every regular cardinal above kappa is omega-strongly measurable in HOD or HOD computes successors correctly). Recent work (exacting cardinals, Bagaria-Goldberg-Lucke 2024) suggests possible refutation
open_case.result: HOD Dichotomy theorem (Woodin 2017) establishes the dichotomy; HOD Conjecture (the 'close' alternative) remains open and recent work threatens it via exacting/ultraexacting cardinals
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; recent arXiv 2411.11568 (Bagaria-Goldberg-Lucke) argues exacting cardinals would refute HOD Conjecture

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims HOD Conjecture is settled (it is not) or claims inaccessibles are provable in ZFC (false)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled non-provability of inaccessibles from open HOD-Conjecture status

trivial_case.description: Consistency strength of a measurable cardinal is strictly above ZFC: if kappa is measurable, then 0# exists, hence V != L, and one can prove Con(ZFC + 'there is a measurable cardinal') is strictly above Con(ZFC)
trivial_case.result: Scott's theorem: if there is a measurable, then V != L; combined with standard equiconsistency arguments
trivial_case.proven_by: Dana Scott (V != L) and Gaifman-Solovay (consistency-strength refinements)
trivial_case.venue: Scott, Bull. Acad. Polon. Sci. 9 (1961), 'Measurable cardinals and constructible sets'

open_case.description: Precise consistency-strength placement of Vopěnka's principle relative to other large cardinals: known to lie between extendible and almost-huge, but precise calibration with intermediate notions (e.g., C^{(n)}-extendibles, supercompact-of-Laver) remains under refinement
open_case.result: Bagaria's C^{(n)}-cardinal hierarchy (Archive Math Logic 2012) gives partial calibration; precise Vopěnka strength relative to specific large-cardinal axioms still being refined
open_case.status: OPEN refinements as of 2026; Vopěnka equivalent to 'C^{(n)}-extendibles for all n' but finer placements active

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Vopěnka strength is exactly characterized (it is up to a level); conflates measurable existence (settled-strict-above-ZFC) with Vopěnka calibration (open refinements)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled measurable-vs-ZFC strength jump from open precise Vopěnka calibration

trivial_case.description: Lusin's separation theorem: any two disjoint analytic sets in a Polish space can be separated by a Borel set
trivial_case.result: Classical descriptive-set-theoretic theorem, proved by Souslin's operation analysis
trivial_case.venue: Lusin, 'Leçons sur les ensembles analytiques' (1930); separation theorem in 1927 papers

open_case.description: Whether the projective separation property at level Pi^1_n for n >= 3 holds in ZFC alone (without determinacy/large cardinal hypotheses); equivalently, whether ZFC proves the Pi^1_3 separation property
open_case.result: Under PD, projective separation holds with periodicity (Moschovakis); in ZFC alone, Pi^1_2 separation holds (Kondo's uniformization theorem 1939) but Pi^1_n for n >= 3 requires hypotheses beyond ZFC
open_case.status: OPEN: precise level at which separation requires large cardinals/determinacy is studied but not fully classified in ZFC alone

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims projective separation at Pi^1_3 follows from ZFC; conflates settled analytic separation (Lusin 1927) with open higher-projective levels
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish ZFC-provable analytic separation from open higher-projective levels

trivial_case.description: Under AD, every set of reals has the Baire property and is Lebesgue measurable; the Solovay model ZF+DC+'every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable' is consistent relative to an inaccessible cardinal
trivial_case.result: Solovay's construction via Levy collapse of an inaccessible; Mycielski-Steinhaus AD-implies-LM/BP
trivial_case.venue: Solovay, Annals of Math 92 (1970), 'A model in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable'

open_case.description: Whether AD_R (determinacy of all real-payoff games) + DC has the same consistency strength as 'AD_R + Theta is regular', and the precise calibration of long-game determinacy at length omega^2 with Woodin-cardinal hierarchies
open_case.result: Woodin established AD_R + Theta-regular has measurable-Woodin-cardinal-equivalence-strength; partial-progress on length-omega^2 (Aguilera, Mueller, Schlutzenberg, Trang 2020-2024); full classification open
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; arXiv 1906.11949 represents the 'long projective determinacy' partial progress

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims AD_R + Theta-regular has the same calibration as ordinary AD (it does not); conflates Solovay model (settled) with AD_R-strength refinements (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Solovay model and AD-implications from open AD_R-Theta-regular calibration

trivial_case.description: K^c (the core model below a measurable) is constructible in ZFC + 'no inner model with a measurable cardinal'; covering lemma holds for K below 0†
trivial_case.result: Mitchell-Steel core-model construction K^c; Dodd-Jensen covering
trivial_case.proven_by: William Mitchell, John Steel, Anthony Dodd, Ronald Jensen
trivial_case.venue: Mitchell-Steel, 'Fine Structure and Iteration Trees', Lect. Notes Logic 3 (1994)

open_case.description: Existence of a fine-structural canonical inner model with a Woodin cardinal that is also a limit of Woodin cardinals + measurable, with full iterability proof; the 'core model induction' beyond AD_R + Theta-regular is open
open_case.result: Sargsyan's hod-mouse construction below a Woodin-limit-of-Woodins (2014, Memoirs AMS); above this level open; Nairian models (Sargsyan-Blue-Larson, arXiv 2501.18958, 2025) make further partial progress
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; active hod-mouse / inner-model-induction program

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims hod-mouse construction is complete through supercompact (false — only partial); conflates K^c (settled below measurable) with hod-mouse program (open above current frontier)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled core-model below measurable from open hod-mouse frontier

trivial_case.description: Friedberg-Muchnik theorem: there exist incomparable c.e. (recursively enumerable) Turing degrees strictly between 0 and 0', solving Post's problem
trivial_case.result: Priority method: simultaneous finite-injury construction of A and B with A not <=_T B and B not <=_T A
trivial_case.venue: Friedberg, PNAS 43 (1957); Muchnik, Doklady 108 (1956)

open_case.description: Whether the c.e. Turing degrees R have any non-trivial automorphism (the c.e. degrees automorphism problem); equivalently, the bi-interpretability conjecture for R
open_case.result: Slaman-Woodin program established many definability results; Nies-Shore-Slaman (1998) showed every relation invariant under automorphisms is definable; existence of a single non-trivial automorphism remains open
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims c.e. degrees are rigid (proved-no-automorphisms) when only partial automorphism-invariance results are known; conflates Post's problem (settled) with automorphism question (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Post's-problem solution from open c.e.-degree-automorphism question

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Square principles and approachability at successors of singulars

trivial_case.description: If V=L, then square_kappa holds for every uncountable cardinal kappa (Jensen's square principle); hence square_aleph_omega holds in L, and similarly approachability AP(aleph_{omega+1}) holds
trivial_case.result: Jensen's fine-structural analysis of L; square_kappa is a global combinatorial principle in L
trivial_case.venue: Jensen, 'The fine structure of the constructible hierarchy', Annals of Math. Logic 4 (1972)

open_case.description: Whether the failure of square_aleph_omega together with SCH-failure at aleph_omega is consistent at strictly weaker large-cardinal strength than currently known (current: subcompact / strong compactness); precise consistency strength still being refined
open_case.result: Magidor-Shelah, Cummings-Foreman-Magidor, Sinapova-Unger (2018): consistency from supercompact; precise lower bound below subcompact open
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; precise consistency-strength calibration of square-failure + SCH-failure at aleph_omega is active

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims precise consistency strength of square-failure + SCH-failure at aleph_omega is known (only upper-bounded); conflates square in L (settled) with open consistency-calibration
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled square-in-L from open precise-strength of combined square+SCH failures

trivial_case.description: Hindman's theorem (HT): for every finite coloring of N, there is an infinite set X subset N such that all finite sums of distinct elements of X are monochromatic. HT implies ACA_0 over RCA_0 (Blass-Hirst-Simpson 1987)
trivial_case.result: Blass-Hirst-Simpson via syntactic-arithmetic reduction; HT is known to lie between ACA_0 and ACA_0^+
trivial_case.proven_by: Andreas Blass, Jeffry Hirst, Stephen Simpson
trivial_case.venue: Blass-Hirst-Simpson, 'Logical analysis of some theorems of combinatorics and topological dynamics', Contemp. Math. 65 (1987)
trivial_case.status: PROVEN (HT lower bound at ACA_0 and upper bound at ACA_0^+)

open_case.description: The exact reverse-mathematics strength of Hindman's theorem: whether HT is equivalent to ACA_0 or to ACA_0^+ over RCA_0; Towsner and others have made partial progress, but the precise calibration is open
open_case.result: Towsner (2012), Dzhafarov-Hirschfeldt-Lerman, and recent refinements narrow but do not close the ACA_0 vs ACA_0^+ gap
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; major problem in reverse mathematics

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims HT is equivalent to ACA_0 over RCA_0 (only one direction known precisely); conflates Hindman-implies-ACA_0 (settled) with precise-strength-determination (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Hindman-implies-ACA_0 from open precise upper-bound calibration

trivial_case.description: MM++ implies Woodin's Pmax axiom (*); equivalently, MM++ decides H(omega_2)-theory in a Pmax-canonical way
trivial_case.result: Aspero-Schindler proof using a tower-forcing-+-iteration argument; settles a question open since the 1990s
trivial_case.venue: Annals of Mathematics 193 (2021), 'Martin's Maximum^++ implies Woodin's axiom (*)'

open_case.description: Whether PFA alone (without MM++) implies (*) — equivalently, whether PFA is sufficient to decide H(omega_2)-theory in the Pmax-canonical way; the question of whether PFA is consistent with ¬(*) is also open
open_case.result: Aspero-Schindler proof crucially uses MM++ stationary-set-preservation, which PFA lacks; whether the result extends to PFA is conjecturally negative but not proven
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims PFA implies (*) (false — only MM++ is known to); conflates settled MM++-implies-(*) with open PFA-vs-(*) question
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled MM++-implies-(*) (Aspero-Schindler 2021) from open PFA-vs-(*) question

trivial_case.description: Kunen's inconsistency theorem: in ZFC, there is no nontrivial elementary embedding j : V -> V; equivalently, no Reinhardt cardinal exists in ZFC
trivial_case.result: Kunen used the Erdos-Hajnal partition theorem to derive contradiction from j : V -> V in ZFC
trivial_case.venue: Kunen, J. Symb. Logic 36 (1971), 'Elementary embeddings and infinitary combinatorics'

open_case.description: Whether ZF + 'there is a Reinhardt cardinal' is consistent (without Choice); whether ZF + Berkeley cardinals is consistent; whether choiceless large cardinals beyond ZFC have a coherent inner-model theory
open_case.result: Bagaria-Koellner-Woodin (2019) established consistency strength of various choiceless large cardinals relative to each other; absolute consistency relative to ZF (or ZF + small fragments) remains open. Goldberg's recent work (2021-2024) on choiceless cardinals continues
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Reinhardt cardinals are inconsistent in ZF (only proven in ZFC); conflates Kunen ZFC-inconsistency (settled) with ZF-consistency (open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled Kunen-inconsistency-in-ZFC from open ZF-without-Choice version

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Universally Baire sets and generic absoluteness

trivial_case.description: Under proper-class-many Woodin cardinals, the theory of L(R) is generically absolute: for every set forcing P, L(R)^V == L(R)^{V[G]} elementarily (for projective formulas, and more)
trivial_case.result: Woodin's generic-absoluteness theorem, using stationary-tower forcing and Sigma^2_1-absoluteness
trivial_case.venue: Steel, Notes on Woodin's lectures; published Woodin, 'Supercompact cardinals, sets of reals, and weakly homogeneous trees', PNAS 85 (1988)

open_case.description: Whether 'every set of reals is universally Baire' has the same consistency strength as 'AD_R + Theta is regular' or as 'AD^+ + V=L(P(R))'; the precise calibration of universal-Baireness with the Solovay-Theta hierarchy is open
open_case.result: Larson-Sargsyan-Trang (2024) established partial reductions; Schlutzenberg-Trang on derived models. Precise equiconsistency of all-sets-uB with specific AD+ axioms not fully resolved
open_case.status: OPEN as of 2026; arXiv 2106.04244 'consistency strength of determinacy when all sets are universally Baire' is partial progress

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims all-sets-uB equiconsistency with specific AD-variants is settled (only partial reductions known); conflates settled L(R)-generic-absoluteness with open uB-equiconsistency
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: substrate must distinguish settled L(R)-generic-absoluteness (Woodin 1988) from open all-sets-uB equiconsistency

attribution.probe: Who proved the consistency of the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis with ZFC, and where was it first announced?
attribution.expected_answer: Kurt Gödel announced the relative consistency of GCH (and AC) with ZF in PNAS Vol. 24 No. 12 (December 1938), pp. 556-557, via the inner model L of constructible sets. The full proof appeared as the 1940 Princeton monograph 'The Consistency of the Continuum Hypothesis'.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Consistency of GCH with ZF (constructible universe L)
attribution_tuples.venue: PNAS Vol. 24 No. 12, pp. 556-557

attribution_tuples.theorem: Consistency of GCH with ZF (full monograph)
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Mathematics Studies 3, Princeton University Press

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing the consistency direction to Cohen (Cohen proved the consistency of NEGATION of CH in 1963 — the OPPOSITE direction of the independence)
wrong year 1939 or 1940 for the PNAS announcement (the announcement was 1938; only the monograph is 1940)
venue confusion citing Annals of Mathematics or JSL instead of PNAS / Annals of Math Studies
attributing to von Neumann or Bernays who developed adjacent set-theoretic frameworks but did not prove this

attribution.probe: Who proved the consistency of the negation of the Continuum Hypothesis with ZFC?
attribution.expected_answer: Paul J. Cohen, by introducing the method of forcing. The two-part announcement appeared as 'The Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis' in PNAS Vol. 50 (1963) pp. 1143-1148 and 'The Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis, II' in PNAS Vol. 51 (1964) pp. 105-110.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Consistency of ¬CH with ZFC (forcing)
Consistency of ¬CH with ZFC, Part II
attribution_tuples.venue: PNAS Vol. 51, pp. 105-110

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Gödel (who proved the OPPOSITE direction — consistency of CH itself)
wrong year 1962 or 1965 (the two-part PNAS publication was 1963 and 1964 specifically)
venue confusion citing Annals of Mathematics or Journal of Symbolic Logic instead of PNAS
attributing to Solovay or Tennenbaum (who developed iterated forcing later, on Souslin's problem, not CH)

attribution.probe: Who proved Borel determinacy in ZFC?
attribution.expected_answer: Donald A. Martin, in 'Borel determinacy', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 102 No. 2 (1975), pp. 363-371. The proof requires the axiom of replacement essentially (per a result of H. Friedman).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Borel sets are determined (in ZFC)
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Mathematics Vol. 102 No. 2

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Martin and Steel (who proved PROJECTIVE determinacy from Woodin cardinals — a different theorem, in 1989)
wrong year 1973 or 1985 (Borel determinacy was 1975; the simplified proof is 1985 in JSL but original is 1975)
venue confusion citing Journal of Symbolic Logic instead of Annals of Math
attributing to Friedman (Friedman proved that replacement is needed — meta-result, not the determinacy theorem)

attribution.probe: Who proved Morley's categoricity theorem (a countable first-order theory categorical in some uncountable cardinality is categorical in all uncountable cardinalities)?
attribution.expected_answer: Michael Morley, 'Categoricity in power', Transactions of the American Mathematical Society Vol. 114 (1965), pp. 514-538, with announcement in PNAS Vol. 49 (1963), pp. 213-216.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Morley's categoricity theorem
attribution_tuples.venue: Transactions AMS Vol. 114, pp. 514-538

attribution_tuples.theorem: Morley's categoricity theorem (announcement)
attribution_tuples.venue: PNAS Vol. 49, pp. 213-216

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Łoś (who CONJECTURED the result; Morley proved it)
attributing to Shelah (who later extended/refined to uncountable languages and stability theory)
wrong year 1962 or 1967 (the Transactions paper is 1965)
venue confusion citing Journal of Symbolic Logic or Annals of Mathematics

attribution.probe: Who solved Post's problem by exhibiting two computably enumerable sets of incomparable Turing degree?
attribution.expected_answer: Independently, Richard M. Friedberg and Albert A. Muchnik, using the finite-injury priority method. Muchnik published in Doklady Akademii Nauk SSSR vol. 108 (1956), pp. 194-197; Friedberg in PNAS vol. 43 (1957), pp. 236-238.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Existence of incomparable c.e. Turing degrees (Post's problem)
attribution_tuples.venue: Doklady Akademii Nauk SSSR Vol. 108

attribution_tuples.theorem: Existence of incomparable c.e. Turing degrees (Post's problem)
attribution_tuples.venue: PNAS Vol. 43

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing solely to Friedberg or solely to Muchnik (the two solutions are independent and contemporaneous)
attributing to Post (who POSED the problem in 1944 but did not solve it)
wrong years 1954 or 1958 (Muchnik 1956, Friedberg 1957)
venue confusion citing JSL instead of Doklady / PNAS

attribution.probe: Who proved the consistency of Martin's Axiom together with the negation of the Continuum Hypothesis, by introducing iterated forcing?
attribution.expected_answer: Robert M. Solovay and Stanley Tennenbaum, in 'Iterated Cohen extensions and Souslin's problem', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 94 No. 2 (1971), pp. 201-245. The same paper establishes consistency of Souslin's hypothesis.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Consistency of MA + ¬CH; consistency of Souslin's hypothesis
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Mathematics Vol. 94 No. 2, pp. 201-245

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Martin alone (Martin formulated MA but Solovay-Tennenbaum proved its consistency with ¬CH)
wrong year 1965 (work circulated earlier but the published paper is 1971)
venue confusion citing Israel Journal of Mathematics or JSL instead of Annals of Math
attributing the Souslin half to Jensen (Jensen proved the OTHER consistency direction — ¬SH from V=L via diamond)

attribution.probe: Who proved that the Whitehead problem for abelian groups of size aleph_1 is independent of ZFC?
attribution.expected_answer: Saharon Shelah, who proved both directions: 'Infinite abelian groups, Whitehead problem and some constructions', Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 18 (1974), pp. 243-256, with the non-free direction in the companion 'Whitehead groups may not be free, even assuming CH, I', Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28 (1977).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Whitehead problem independent of ZFC (V=L gives Whitehead implies free; MA+¬CH gives counterexample)
attribution_tuples.venue: Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 18, pp. 243-256

attribution_tuples.theorem: Whitehead groups may not be free even assuming CH
attribution_tuples.venue: Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Whitehead himself (who posed the problem; he did not show independence)
attributing to Eklof (who wrote a popular exposition for the AMS Notices/Monthly but did not prove it)
wrong year 1972 or 1978 (the original ZFC-independence paper is 1974)
venue confusion citing Annals of Mathematics or JSL instead of Israel Journal of Mathematics

attribution.probe: Who proved that, assuming an inaccessible cardinal, there is a model of ZF+DC in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable, has the Baire property, and the perfect set property?
attribution.expected_answer: Robert M. Solovay, 'A model of set-theory in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 92 (1970), pp. 1-56.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Solovay's model: every set of reals Lebesgue measurable (from inaccessible)
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Mathematics Vol. 92, pp. 1-56

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Shelah (Shelah later showed the inaccessible is NEEDED for the Lebesgue conclusion — different theorem, 1984)
wrong year 1965 or 1972 (the published paper is 1970, although work was done in the 1960s)
venue confusion citing Israel Journal of Mathematics or JSL instead of Annals of Math
incorrectly stating no inaccessible is needed (an inaccessible IS used; Shelah later showed it is necessary for Lebesgue measurability of all sets)

attribution.probe: Who proved Kunen's inconsistency theorem (that there is no nontrivial elementary embedding j : V -> V in ZFC)?
attribution.expected_answer: Kenneth Kunen, 'Elementary embeddings and infinitary combinatorics', Journal of Symbolic Logic Vol. 36 No. 3 (1971), pp. 407-413. This result rules out Reinhardt cardinals in ZFC.

attribution_tuples.theorem: No nontrivial elementary j : V -> V in ZFC (Kunen inconsistency)
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of Symbolic Logic Vol. 36 No. 3, pp. 407-413

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Reinhardt (Reinhardt PROPOSED the embedding axiom; Kunen REFUTED it in ZFC)
wrong year 1970 or 1973 (the JSL paper is 1971)
venue confusion citing Annals of Math or Annals of Mathematical Logic instead of JSL
incorrectly stating it works without choice (the proof essentially uses AC; consistency in ZF without AC is open)

attribution.probe: Who proved that IP = PSPACE (interactive proofs equal polynomial space)?
attribution.expected_answer: Adi Shamir, 'IP = PSPACE', Journal of the ACM Vol. 39 No. 4 (October 1992), pp. 869-877; originally announced at FOCS 1990. The matching lower bound IP ⊇ PSPACE used the Lund-Fortnow-Karloff-Nisan arithmetization of QBF.

attribution_tuples.theorem: IP = PSPACE
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of the ACM Vol. 39 No. 4, pp. 869-877

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing solely to Lund-Fortnow-Karloff-Nisan (who proved coNP ⊆ IP and #P ⊆ IP, the precursor; Shamir extended to PSPACE)
wrong year 1990 for the J. ACM publication (1990 was FOCS; J. ACM is 1992)
venue confusion citing SIAM J. Computing or STOC instead of J. ACM
attributing to Goldwasser-Micali-Rackoff (who DEFINED interactive proofs, did not prove IP=PSPACE)

attribution.probe: Who proved Projective Determinacy from infinitely many Woodin cardinals?
attribution.expected_answer: Donald A. Martin and John R. Steel, 'A proof of projective determinacy', Journal of the American Mathematical Society Vol. 2 No. 1 (1989), pp. 71-125.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Projective Determinacy follows from infinitely many Woodin cardinals
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of the AMS Vol. 2 No. 1, pp. 71-125

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing solely to Martin (who proved Borel determinacy in 1975; Projective Determinacy is the joint Martin-Steel 1989)
attributing to Woodin (Woodin developed Pmax and the optimal-determinacy proof from AD+; the PD-from-Woodins result is Martin-Steel)
wrong year 1985 or 1992 (the J. AMS publication is 1989)
venue confusion citing Annals of Math or Annals of Pure and Applied Logic instead of J. AMS

attribution.probe: Who proved the consistency of Martin's Maximum (MM)?
attribution.expected_answer: Matthew Foreman, Menachem Magidor, and Saharon Shelah, 'Martin's Maximum, saturated ideals, and nonregular ultrafilters. Part I', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 127 No. 1 (1988), pp. 1-47.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Consistency of Martin's Maximum (MM) from a supercompact
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Mathematics Vol. 127 No. 1, pp. 1-47

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing the consistency to Asperó-Schindler (who proved MM++ implies (*) in 2021 — a different result downstream of MM)
wrong year 1987 or 1989 (the Annals paper is 1988; Part II is 1988 vol 127 no 3)
venue confusion citing Israel Journal of Mathematics or JSL instead of Annals of Math
attributing solely to Foreman or solely to Shelah (the consistency proof is the joint Foreman-Magidor-Shelah)

attribution.probe: Who proved the consistency of the failure of the Singular Cardinals Hypothesis at aleph_omega from a supercompact cardinal?
attribution.expected_answer: Menachem Magidor, in two papers from 1977: 'On the singular cardinals problem I', Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28 (1977), pp. 1-31 (constructing a model where 2^{aleph_omega} > aleph_{omega+1} with aleph_omega strong limit), and 'On the singular cardinals problem II', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 106 (1977), pp. 517-547.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Failure of GCH at aleph_omega from supercompact (SCH failure at aleph_omega)
attribution_tuples.venue: Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28, pp. 1-31

capability_gap.object_class: Forcing notion (Cohen forcing for adding a real)

object_specification.type: Cohen forcing P = (2^{<omega}, supseteq)

instance.underlying_set: {s : s is a function from a finite ordinal n in omega into 2}; presented by the recursion S_0 = {<>}, S_{n+1} = {s ^ <0> : s in S_n} cup {s ^ <1> : s in S_n}, underlying_set = union_{n<omega} S_n
instance.order: p <= q iff dom(q) subseteq dom(p) and p restricted to dom(q) equals q
instance.antichain_specification: an antichain is a set A of pairwise-incompatible conditions; max antichain size is aleph_0 (countable)

structural_metadata.completeness: not sigma-closed (the descending sequence p_n = 0^n is not bounded below by a condition)
structural_metadata.regularity: homogeneous (for any p, q in P there is an automorphism phi with phi(p) = q via bit-flip composed with rebasing)

verification_methods.is_ccc: any antichain is at most countable since |P| = aleph_0
verification_methods.preserves_cardinals: follows from ccc by Solovay's theorem
verification_methods.adds_real: the union of the generic filter G is a function omega -> 2 not in V
verification_methods.homogeneous: explicit bit-flip automorphism phi_t(p)(n) = p(n) XOR t(n) for t in 2^{dom(p)}

object_specification.type: Sacks forcing P_S = (PerfectTrees(2^{<omega}), supseteq)

instance.underlying_set: {T subseteq 2^{<omega} : T is a tree (closed under initial segments) AND for every s in T there exist t_0, t_1 in T with s subseteq t_0, s subseteq t_1, t_0 incomparable with t_1}
instance.order: T <= S iff T subseteq S
instance.splitting_predicate: s is a splitting node of T iff s ^ <0> in T AND s ^ <1> in T
instance.fusion_sequence_definition: a fusion sequence is (T_n, F_n) with T_{n+1} <= T_n and F_n subseteq splitnodes(T_n) finite, F_n subseteq F_{n+1}, every node in F_n is a splitting node in T_{n+1}

structural_metadata.minimal_real_property: the generic real has minimal V-degree (no real strictly between V and V[s])

verification_methods.is_proper: Axiom A with fusion implies proper (Baumgartner-Laver)
verification_methods.is_omega_omega_bounding: any name for f: omega -> omega is fusion-decided to lie below a ground-model g
verification_methods.minimal_real: if x in V[s] non-trivial then s in V[x] (Sacks 1971)
verification_methods.homogeneous: for T, S perfect trees, the canonical isomorphism via splitting-node bijection lifts to a poset automorphism

object_specification.type: Mathias forcing P_M = ({(a, A) : a in [omega]^{<omega}, A in [omega]^omega, max(a) < min(A)}, <=_M)

instance.underlying_set: pairs (a, A) where a is a finite subset of omega (presented as the strictly increasing tuple of its elements), A is an infinite subset of omega, and every element of a is strictly less than every element of A

verification_methods.ramsey_property: (a, A) forces 'union of generic stems is c-homogeneous' for any c in V

capability_gap.object_class: Forcing notion (Prikry forcing for a measurable cardinal)

object_specification.type: Prikry forcing P_kappa = ({(s, A) : s in [kappa]^{<omega}, s strictly increasing, A in U, min(A) > max(s)}, <=_P) where U is a normal ultrafilter on measurable kappa

instance.parameter: kappa is measurable with normal ultrafilter U on kappa (assumed given)
instance.underlying_set: pairs (s, A) where s = (alpha_0 < alpha_1 < ... < alpha_{n-1}) is a finite increasing sequence from kappa and A in U with min(A) > alpha_{n-1} (or A = kappa if s = empty)
instance.order: (t, B) <=_P (s, A) iff s is an initial segment of t, B subseteq A, and {t(i) : i >= |s|} subseteq A
instance.prikry_property_statement: for any sentence phi in the forcing language and any (s, A), there is B subseteq A with B in U such that (s, B) decides phi

verification_methods.preserves_cardinals: Prikry property + U-normality imply no new bounded subsets of kappa, hence cardinals preserved (Prikry 1970)
verification_methods.changes_cofinality: the union of generic stems is an omega-sequence cofinal in kappa
verification_methods.prikry_property_check: for each (s, A) and phi, define A_0 = {alpha in A : exists B subseteq A setminus (alpha+1), B in U, (s ^ <alpha>, B) decides phi}; show A_0 in U by normality

capability_gap.object_class: Forcing notion (Magidor forcing for changing cof(kappa) to uncountable)

object_specification.type: Magidor forcing P_M(kappa, lambda) for changing cof(kappa) to lambda < kappa, using a coherent sequence of measures

instance.parameters: kappa with a coherent sequence of normal measures U = <U_alpha : alpha < lambda> where U_alpha concentrates on {beta < kappa : o(beta) = alpha} (Mitchell order)
instance.underlying_set: conditions are pairs (f, F) where f is a partial function from lambda to kappa with |dom(f)| < lambda, increasing on its domain, and F is a function on lambda \ dom(f) such that F(alpha) in U_alpha for every alpha in dom(F), and the F-image is bounded compatibly with f
instance.order: (g, G) <= (f, F) iff dom(f) subseteq dom(g), g restricted to dom(f) = f, for alpha in dom(g) \ dom(f) we have g(alpha) in F(alpha), and G(alpha) subseteq F(alpha) for alpha in dom(G) cap dom(F)
instance.maximum_element: (empty, F_max) where F_max(alpha) = {beta < kappa : o(beta) = alpha}
instance.coherence_requirement: the ultrafilter sequence must be coherent: if j_alpha : V -> M_alpha is the embedding from U_alpha then j_alpha(U) restricted to alpha equals U restricted to alpha

structural_metadata.consistency_strength: o(kappa) >= lambda + 1 (Mitchell order at least lambda+1)

verification_methods.cofinality_change: the union of dom-extensions yields a cofinal lambda-sequence in kappa

capability_gap.object_class: Tree (Suslin tree on omega_1, diamond-based construction)

object_specification.type: Suslin tree T on omega_1: a tree of height omega_1, all levels countable, with no uncountable chain and no uncountable antichain

instance.construction_under_diamond: assume <>_{omega_1}; let <S_alpha : alpha < omega_1> be a diamond sequence (each S_alpha subseteq alpha and for every X subseteq omega_1, {alpha : X cap alpha = S_alpha} is stationary)
instance.level_construction: Level T_0 = {<>}; level T_{alpha+1} = {x ^ <0>, x ^ <1> : x in T_alpha}; for limit alpha define T_alpha by selecting cofinal branches in T cap alpha by case-analysis on whether S_alpha codes an antichain through T cap alpha
instance.limit_step_specification: if S_alpha codes a maximal antichain A in T cap alpha, then for each x in T cap alpha extending some element of A pick a branch through T cap alpha containing x and put its limit in T_alpha; if S_alpha does not code an antichain, fill T_alpha with all cofinal branches
instance.underlying_set: T = union_{alpha < omega_1} T_alpha; each T_alpha countable
instance.tree_order: x <_T y iff x is a proper initial segment of y (T is a subtree of 2^{<omega_1})

verification_methods.no_uncountable_antichain: diamond ensures any potential uncountable antichain A is anticipated as S_alpha for stationary alpha; the limit step seals A at level alpha so no extension above alpha avoids A
verification_methods.no_uncountable_chain: any chain through T projects to a function omega_1 -> 2 of unbounded length, but levels are countable so cofinal chain forces uncountable antichain (contradiction by previous)
verification_methods.all_levels_countable: by construction (countable splitting at each level)

capability_gap.object_class: Tree (Aronszajn tree on omega_1)

object_specification.type: Aronszajn tree T on omega_1: tree of height omega_1, all levels countable, no uncountable branch (note: Aronszajn trees provably exist in ZFC; Suslin requires more)

instance.explicit_construction_via_finite_partial: Level T_alpha = {f : f is a finite partial injective function alpha -> Q with cofinal range in Q cap (-infty, q) for some q in Q}; specifically T_alpha consists of strictly-increasing finite-to-rationals partial injections with bounded sup
instance.tree_order: f <_T g iff f subseteq g (g extends f)
instance.construction_recursion: successor: T_{alpha+1} = {f cup {(alpha, q)} : f in T_alpha, q in Q, q > sup(range(f)), q < bound(f)+1}; limit alpha: T_alpha = {union_n f_n : (f_n) is a cofinal sequence of compatible elements with bounded suprema}
instance.specialization: T is special: T = union_n T_n where each T_n is an antichain (Q-rationals provide the specialization function)

verification_methods.no_uncountable_branch: any branch B of height omega_1 would project to a strictly-increasing function omega_1 -> Q with bounded sup at every countable stage, but Q is countable so B would force injection omega_1 -> Q (contradiction)
verification_methods.all_levels_countable: by construction; each level a subset of finite partial functions with bounded range so countable
verification_methods.specialization: the bound-of-range function f |-> bound(f) (after rounding to finite Q-precision) gives a countable partition into antichains

structural_metadata.gap_with_substrate: Same as cap_017 plus specific support for special-tree witness (countable partition into antichains). Need Specialization primitive: an antichain partition Callable[Tree, List[Antichain]].

capability_gap.object_class: Tree (Kurepa tree on omega_1 with aleph_2 cofinal branches)

object_specification.type: Kurepa tree T on omega_1: tree of height omega_1, all levels countable, with at least aleph_2 many cofinal branches

instance.construction_in_L: in V=L use diamond+ (or directly the L-structure) to construct T; at limit stages use the L-canonical well-order to select aleph_2-many distinct cofinal branches arising from L-definable bookkeeping
instance.level_specification: T_alpha subseteq 2^alpha countable, with the property that for each x in T cap alpha, x extends to at least one cofinal branch; the construction ensures the branch-set has cardinality aleph_2
instance.branch_set_specification: Branch(T) = {b : b is a cofinal chain through T}; |Branch(T)| >= aleph_2
instance.consistency_status: Kurepa Hypothesis (KH) = 'there is a Kurepa tree' is independent of ZFC: holds in L, fails after Levy-collapsing an inaccessible to omega_2
instance.explicit_branch_indexing_in_L: in L, branches correspond to L-definable subsets of omega_1 of a specific Sigma_1-definability rank; the indexing is by ordinals < omega_2

structural_metadata.consistency_strength_of_negation: inaccessible cardinal (Silver: Not-KH equiconsistent with inaccessible)

verification_methods.branch_count: explicit aleph_2-indexed family of L-definable cofinal branches
verification_methods.kurepa_definition: verify height + level-countable + branch >= aleph_2 jointly

capability_gap.object_class: Computable structure (Kleene's O, the system of recursive ordinal notations)

object_specification.type: Kleene's O: the set of natural-number indices for recursive ordinals, with a partial order |O| extending well-foundedness

instance.definition_recursive: O is the smallest set such that (a) 1 in O with |1| = 0; (b) if a in O with |a| = alpha, then 2^a in O with |2^a| = alpha + 1; (c) if e is an index for a total computable function such that for all n, phi_e(n) in O and phi_e(n) <_O phi_e(n+1), then 3 * 5^e in O with |3 * 5^e| = sup_n |phi_e(n)|
instance.partial_order: a <_O b is the transitive closure of: (i) a <_O 2^a; (ii) for each n, phi_e(n) <_O 3 * 5^e
instance.ordinal_height: the supremum of {|a| : a in O} is omega_1^{CK}, the Church-Kleene ordinal (least non-recursive ordinal)
instance.explicit_index_examples: 1 codes 0; 2^1 codes 1; 2^{2^1} codes 2; 3 * 5^e codes omega when phi_e(n) = 2^{2^{...^1}} (n-fold tower); etc.
instance.non_uniqueness: |.| is well-defined but the index encoding is not unique: many distinct indices a, b in O have |a| = |b|

structural_metadata.computability_status: the set O is Pi_1_1-complete; not arithmetical
structural_metadata.uniformity: the function n |-> |n| (when defined) is hyperarithmetical but not arithmetical

verification_methods.well_foundedness: well-foundedness of <_O follows from the inductive definition (each clause produces a successor or limit witness)
verification_methods.Pi_1_1_completeness: encode any Pi_1_1 set into O via tree-of-attempts construction (Kleene 1955)
verification_methods.Church_Kleene_height: every recursive ordinal has an index in O; conversely every index codes a recursive ordinal
